title: Rolling Rustic Wood Dresser
summary: A six-drawer dresser on 6-inch caster wheels with a face-frame front and euro-slide drawers — genuinely large caster wheels distinct from this catalog's other rolling dressers.
description:
This dresser combines a plywood carcass, a separately-built face frame, and euro-style drawer slides with genuinely large 6" caster wheels — sized to actually roll over carpet or an uneven floor rather than the small furniture casters used elsewhere in this catalog. **The 2x2 legs need to be straight stock, not just any 2x2** — the plan calls this out directly, since a warped or twisted leg board telegraphs into a rolling dresser far more obviously than it would on a stationary piece.

Build the carcass first with spacer blocks positioned specifically where the drawer slides will later attach, and drill the pocket holes for the top and face frame while those spacers are still accessible — trying to add these holes after the box is fully assembled is much harder. Build the face frame as its own flat, square unit, then line it up with the assembled carcass and attach.

**The center support pieces are explicitly over-building, by the plan's own admission** — they take weight off the center of the dresser and help keep it square, and the plan suggests an optional fifth caster wheel at the center if the dresser will carry unusually heavy loads. Drawer slide cleats go in next with pocket holes, drawers get built and installed on the euro slides, and drawer faces with knobs finish the piece.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet
Euro style drawer slides, bottom corner mount, 16 in
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.material: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet
3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 45°, ends not parallel, longest point measurement.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two sides at 32-1/4" and one top at 62-1/2" from the 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet. Cut six drawer slide spacers at 15-3/4" and four face frame stiles at 29" from the 1x2. Cut four legs at 62-1/2", six center support legs at 30", four drawer slide cleats at 15-3/4" and two face frame center brace at 41" from the 2x2, 8 ft, straight stock. Cut twelve drawer sides at 26-1/2" and twelve drawer front/back at 16" from the 1x8. Cut six drawer bottoms at 16" from the 1/4" plywood, full sheet. Cut six drawer faces at 28-3/4" from the 1x10. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 8 ft 1x2, seven 8 ft 2x2, 8 ft, straight stock, six 8 ft 1x8 and two 8 ft 1x10. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Build the box with drawer spacers
steps.body: Lay each 32-1/4" x 15-3/4" plywood side flat, mark the drawer-slide heights on its inside face, and glue and brad-nail the 15-3/4" 1x2 drawer slide spacers across them — three per side for the three drawers in each column, each spacer running the full 15-3/4" depth so a 16" slide has continuous backing front to back. Get these level, and get the left and right sides matched to each other: a spacer 1/8" out between the two sides is a drawer that will never close square, and you cannot correct it later from the slide adjustment alone. While the spacers are still out in the open, drill the 3/4" pocket holes you will need later — pointing upward along the top edge for the top, and forward along the front edge for the face frame. Drilling those once the box is closed up is the job that ends with the drill body jammed against a panel.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Set the 62-1/2" x 15-3/4" plywood top on the assembled box, line it up so any overhang is even at both ends, and drive 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws up through the holes you predrilled. Work from the middle outward and keep checking the box stays square to the top as you go — the top is the widest single part in the build and it will happily pull a slightly racked carcass into its own shape, which is helpful if the top is square and a disaster if it isn't. Add glue at the joints if you want the top permanent; leave it dry if you would rather be able to replace it later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame flat on a table as its own square unit before it goes anywhere near the carcass: the four 29" 1x2 stiles run vertically, and the two 41" 2x2 center braces have both ends mitered at 45°, not parallel, measured long point to long point. Clamp it flat and check the diagonals — a face frame assembled in place against the box inherits the box's errors instead of correcting them, and the face frame is the part everyone actually looks at. Then stand it against the front of the dresser, line the stiles up with the drawer openings, and fasten either through the pocket holes you predrilled in the spacers or with brad nails and glue. Pin one corner first, square the frame to the box, then pin the rest.

steps.title: Attach the legs and drawer slide cleats
steps.body: Fasten the four 2x2 legs to the corners with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws and glue, and use genuinely straight stock — a twisted 2x2 telegraphs far worse on a piece that rolls, because you feel every rock when you move it. Before you cut to length, note that the cut list gives the legs as 62-1/2", the same as the top's length, against a 32-1/4" carcass; that figure does not survive checking, so decide your own finished height and cut to it rather than taking 62-1/2" at face value. Attach the four 15-3/4" drawer slide cleats the same way, running front to back so the euro slides screw into solid 2x2 instead of plywood edge. Stand the dresser on a flat floor and check all four legs make contact before the glue sets.

steps.title: Add the center support legs
steps.body: Fit the six 30" 2x2 center support legs into the middle of the case, running vertically to carry the centre of a 62-1/2" span that would otherwise sag under six loaded drawers. The plan is open that this is over-building; it also keeps the case square, which matters more on a rolling piece than a stationary one because the frame gets flexed every time you move it. Fasten with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws. If the dresser will carry anything genuinely heavy, add a fifth caster at the centre — with these legs already in place there is solid 2x2 for it to bolt to, which is the reason to decide now rather than later.

steps.title: Build and install the drawers
steps.body: Build each of the six drawer boxes 28" wide by 16" deep. The 16" 1x8 parts run the FULL length as the box's sides and the 26-1/2" parts fit between them, which with 3/4" stock at each end gives the 28" outside width — matching the 28" x 16" 1/4" plywood bottoms exactly, so use a bottom as the squaring gauge for its own box. Be aware the cut list's names are the wrong way round here: the parts labelled 'Drawer front/back' at 16" are the sides that run with the 16" slides, and the 26-1/2" 'Drawer sides' are the front and back. Glue and brad-nail the corners, then glue and brad the bottom on and check the diagonals before it sets. Mount each box on a pair of euro bottom-corner slides, screwing into the 2x2 cleats and the 1x2 spacers rather than into plywood.
materials: Euro style drawer slides, bottom corner mount, 16 in

steps.title: Attach drawer faces, casters, and finish
steps.body: Fit the six 28-3/4" x 9-1/4" drawer faces with an even reveal all round — each face is 3/4" wider than its 28" box, so it stands 3/8" proud at each side, and shimming with a couple of playing cards while you drive the first screw is far more reliable than eyeballing it. Drill for the knobs through the face and the drawer front together so the holes cannot end up offset. Bolt the four 6" casters to the bottom, landing every plate on solid 2x2 leg rather than plywood — these are big enough to actually roll over carpet, which is the entire point of them, but they also transmit the dresser's whole loaded weight through four small plates. Sand, break the edges, and apply primer and paint, or stain and poly, finishing the drawer faces off the dresser so you can reach their edges.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Six-drawer rolling dresser on large caster wheels
